title: Repairing a continuously running WC cistern
subtitle: Diagnose and fix a toilet cistern that runs water into the pan or overflows
excerpt: How to diagnose and fix a WC cistern that continuously runs water; either silently into the pan or audibly into the overflow. Covers the two most common causes: a failed float valve and a failed flap valve (siphon diaphragm or drop valve seal).
sourceNotes: British Standard BS 6465-1: Sanitary installations (code of practice). Collins Complete DIY Manual, Albert Jackson and David Day (1989). Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual (referenced, not reproduced).

glossaryTerms.definition: The valve at the inlet of the cistern that shuts off the water supply when the cistern is full. A ballcock (float ball on a horizontal arm) or a diaphragm float valve (Fluidmaster-style compact unit with a vertical float). When the float valve fails to close — due to a damaged diaphragm or a set float level that is too high — water continues to run over the overflow pipe. The fix is replacing the diaphragm washer or adjusting the float arm height.

The component inside the siphon unit or drop valve that creates the flush: it opens on flush and seals when the cistern refills. On older siphon-flush cisterns, it is a flexible plastic disc (the diaphragm); on modern drop-valve systems (Geberit, Thomas Dudley), it is a rubber seal on a float-operated valve. When the seal deteriorates, water seeps continuously from the cistern into the pan — often silently, only detectable by a dye test.

content.text: A running WC wastes 200 to 800 litres of water per day. You can detect it by the sound of running water, by water in the pan, or by adding food dye to the cistern. If colour appears in the pan within 15 minutes without flushing, the cistern is leaking. The cause is almost always one of two parts: the

Replacement float valve washer (or full float valve if the valve is old; Fluidmaster 400 universal fits most cisterns). Replacement cistern siphon diaphragm (for old siphon-type cisterns; measure the siphon diameter before purchasing: common sizes are 55 mm and 70 mm). Adjustable spanner. Food dye for testing. A pencil to mark the water level. Rubber gloves.

Lift the cistern lid and look. If water runs out of the overflow into the pan or out through an external pipe, the float valve is the problem: it is not shutting off at the right level. If the cistern fills but water trickles into the pan, the diaphragm or drop valve seal is the problem. Add food dye to the cistern water and watch the pan. If colour appears without flushing, water is seeping through.

Turn off the supply at the isolation valve on the supply pipe below the cistern (or at the mains stopcock). Flush the cistern to empty it. On a ballcock: unscrew the cap on the valve body, remove the piston or plunger, and replace the rubber washer on its end. On a diaphragm float valve (Fluidmaster-style): unscrew the top cap, remove the diaphragm assembly, and replace the diaphragm. If the float arm is set too high, bend it down slightly (on an old-style ballcock) or adjust the height clip (on a modern diaphragm valve) so the valve shuts before the water reaches the overflow level. Turn on the supply and check.

Turn off the supply. Flush to empty the cistern. For a siphon-type cistern: disconnect the cistern supply (the bottom pipe) and the cistern from the pan by releasing the wing nuts under the cistern (if close-coupled). Lift out the siphon assembly. Pull the diaphragm off the perforated plate inside the siphon; it is usually held by a clip and peels off. Replace with the correct diameter diaphragm (cut to fit if needed; diaphragm material is sold in packs). Reassemble and refit.

Modern drop valve cisterns (Geberit, Thomas Dudley Turbo) have a large plastic valve at the base of the cistern. If the drop valve seal is perished, the cistern seeps into the pan. The valve assembly slides out of the cistern base (turn and lift on most designs) and the rubber seal is replaceable; order by brand and model number, as seals are not interchangeable between brands.

attrs.symptom: Cistern does not refill after flushing
attrs.cause: Float valve isolation valve is closed, or the float valve is stuck closed.
attrs.fix: Check the isolation valve is open (slot inline with the pipe). If open, the float valve is stuck — tap the valve body gently to free it or replace the valve.

attrs.symptom: Food dye test shows no colour in pan but water sound is audible
attrs.cause: Sound is from the float valve letting water in just before shutting — the cistern is working normally but the float is set close to overflow level.
attrs.fix: Adjust the float arm height to give a larger margin between the full water level and the overflow inlet. The normal full level should be 25 mm below the overflow.
